Facts.text: The Theodosian Code of Roman laws proclaimed in the Eastern Empire (first law reforms since 295)
Khosrau II, the last great Sasanian king, is crowned King of Persia
Zen teacher Ho-tse Shen-hui disputes founder of Northern Ch'an line
Bernardo is elected Pope Eugene III
Peace of Angleur, Liège signed
Duke Philip the Stout forms Council of Flanders
Emperor Charles receives Cardinal Pole in Toledo
Dutch coast hit by heavy storm
Russian troops occupy Polotsk Lithuania
Ferdinand III succeeds Ferdinand II as Holy Roman Emperor
KingCharles IIreports anti-French covenant with Netherlands
Jean Baptiste Lully's opera "Armide", with libretto by Philippe Quinault, premieres in performance by the Paris Opera at the Théâtre du Palais-Royal
Parliament of Paris accepts Pope Clement XI's "Unigenitus" decree
Austria, Prussia and Saxony sign the Treaty of Hubertusburg, marking the end of the French and Indian War and of the Seven Years' War
St. Louis, Missouri founded as a French trading post by Pierre Laclède
First mustard manufactured in America is advertised in Philadelphia
Louis Alexandre Berthier proclaim a Roman Republic after conquering Rome
First US printed ballots are authorized in Pennsylvania
New Jersey becomes last northern state to abolish slavery
Harmony Society officially forms
William Parsons, Earl of Rosse, 1st uses 72 inch (183 cm) reflector telescope  at Birr Castle, Parsonstown, County Offaly, Ireland
Sarah Roberts barred from white school in Boston
Black abolitionists invade Boston courtroom rescuing a fugitive slave
Great Ormond St Hospital for Sick Children, London, admits 1st patient
Fort Point, in San Francisco, California, completed & garrisoned (but has never fired cannon in anger)
Grant's major assault on Ft Donelson, Tennessee
Fire in Rotterdam damages Museum Boijmans Van Beuningen
Johann Strauss' "Blue Danube" waltz premieres in Vienna
Ground broken for Northern Pacific Railway near Duluth, Minnesota
Historic Elm at Boston blown down
US Congress authorizes women lawyers to practise before Supreme Court
SS Dunedin leaves New Zealand for Britain with the first cargo of frozen meat.
Swedish sports club AIK is founded at Biblioteksgatan 8 in Stockholm by Isidor Behrens
23 cm (9 inches) of snow falls on New Orleans
USS Maine sinks in Havana harbor, cause unknown, 258 sailors die
Berlin U-Bahn (Underground railway) opened
First teddy bear introduced in America, made by Morris and Rose Michtom
First Thoroughbred horse race meet at Oaklawn Park in Hot Springs, Arkansas
British Labour Party founded
Schooner 'Fram' reaches latitude 78° 41' S, the farthest south ever by a ship
First avant-garde art show in America opens in NYC
NY Yankees buy Frank "Home Run" Baker from the Athletics for $37,500
San Francisco Public Library (Main Branch at Civic center) dedicated
US army troop ship torpedoed & sunk by Germany off Ireland
American Legion organizes in Paris

A deputation for the South African Indian Congress (SAIC) meets with the Minister of the Interior, Sir Patrick Duncan, and presents him with a memorandum setting out their objections concerning the Class Areas Bill
Brooks Atkinson Theatre opens at 256 W 47th St NYC
Contract air mail service begins in US
Wenona Mustangs beat Toluca Wildcats in Illinois Basketball Tournament in 10 overtimes, 26-22
Spring training site of NY Yankees in St Petersburg is renamed Miller Huggins Field in honor of the team's late manager
American brothers Hubert and Curtis Stevens beat the 8-nation field to win the 2-man bobsleigh gold medal at the Lake Placid Winter Olympics
Australia beat South Africa in cricket by an innings in 5 hrs 53 min playing time
III Winter Olympic Games close at Lake Placid, New York
Karl Radek praises invincible force of German communist party
Social-democratic newspaper "Vorwarts" banned again in Berlin
-60°F (-51°C), Parshall, North Dakota (state record)
Great Britain’s 0-0 tie with the US is enough to clinch their first ever Olympic ice hockey gold medal at the Garmisch-Partenkirchen Winter Games; breaks Canada’s 4 straight Games golden streak
German battleship Bismarck was launched
German U-boat shells Antillian oil refinery
Japanese troops march into Palembang, South Sumatra
World War II: British ruled Singapore surrenders to the Japanese
Women's camp Tamtui on Ambon (Moluccas) hit by allied air raid
891 British bombers attack Berlin in the largest raid by the RAF against the city
Allies begin attack on Axis held Monte Cassino monastery, Italy
KENS TV channel 5 in San Antonio, Texas (CBS) begins broadcasting
WSYR (now WSTM) TV channel 3 in Syracuse, New York (NBC) begins broadcasting
Giant slalom event for men debuts at the Winter Olympics at Oslo Games; Stein Erikson of Norway wins gold ahead of Austrians Christian Pravda and Toni Spiß
First Bevatron particle accelerator operates in Berkeley, California
WRDW TV channel 12 in Augusta, GA (CBS) begins broadcasting
First pilot plant to produce synthetic man-made diamonds is announced
Pirates and KC As cancel an exhibition game in Birmingham, Alabama, due to a local ordinance barring Black players from playing against White players
Urho Kekkonen appointed president of Finland
Andrei Gromyko succeeds Dmitri Shepilov as Soviet Minister of Foreign Affairs
Ice Dance Championship at Paris won by June Markham/Courtney Jones GRB
Ice Pairs Championship at Paris won by Barbara Wagner/Rob Paul of CAN
Sjafroeddin Prawiranegara forms anti-government of Middle Sumatra
Australia beat West Indies 2-1 in one of best Test Cricket series ever

Plane crash kills entire US figure skating team outside Brussels airport in Belgium enroute to World Championships in Prague from NYC; 73 crew and passengers (including 34 American skaters, coaches, officials, and other team members), and a farmer on the ground are lost
Lawsuit is filed on behalf of nine families whose requests for children to be transferred to newer school facility in Englewood, New Jersey; parents charge denial was "racial segregation"
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Ken Lynch records "Misery," 1st Lennon-McCartney song by someone else
The Beatles' "Meet the Beatles!" album goes #1 & stays #1 for 11 weeks
Red Maple Leaf Flag becomes the official flag of Canada
Kees Verkerk becomes world champion all-round skater
D66 (D'66) wins 7 seats in Dutch 2nd Chamber
First anti-bootleg recording laws are enacted
Longest dream (REM sleep) on record is experienced by Bill Carskadon in Chicago lasting 2 hours and 23 minutes
ABA basketball Anaheim Amigos Les Salvage scores 10 3-point baskets in game vs Denver Rockets
Biathlon 4 x 7.5k relay debuts at the Grenoble Winter Olympics with the Soviet Union taking the gold medal ahead of Norway and Sweden
Fred Anton Maier of Norway skates a world record 7:22.4 in winning the 5,000m at the Grenoble Winter Olympics; Dutch 1,500m champion Kees Verkerk is just 0.8s behind for silver
WVUT TV channel 22 in Vincennes, Indiana (PBS) begins broadcasting
Dominican DC-9 crashes into sea at Santo Domingo, kills 102
KAMU TV channel 15 in College Station, TX (PBS) begins broadcasting
Nationalists disrupt UN session on Congo
A British soldier dies 7 days after being mortally wounded in an Irish Republican Army attack in North Ireland
After 1,200 years Great Britain abandons pence & shilling system for decimal currency
Dimitrios Papadopoulos becomes metropolitan of Imbros and Tenedos
Newly-formed NHL franchise New York Islanders hire Bill Torrey as their first General Manager
President Velasco Ibarra of Ecuador deposed for 4th time
Sound recordings receive US federal copyright protection for the first time
Friendsville Academy (Tenn) ends 138-game basketball losing streak
USSR launches Prognoz 3 to study the Sun (589/200,300 km)
XII Winter Olympic Games close in Innsbruck, Austria
Social-democrats win Danish parliamentary election
England all out 64 for 1st loss to NZ in cricket (Geoffrey Boycott captain)
Zaire revises its constitution
Paul Shirley (21) of Australia, sucked a lifesaver for 4 hrs 40 mins
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
East German speed skater Karin Enke wins first of 3 career Olympic gold medals with victory in the 500m at Lake Placid Winter; wins 1,000m & 1,500m in Sarajevo (1984)
Soviet cross-country skier Raisa Smetanina claims her 3rd of 4 career Olympic gold medals in 5k event at Lake Placid; also wins 10k and relay gold (Innsbruck 1976) and relay (Albertville 1992)
23rd Daytona 500:Richard Pettywins record 7th title at Daytona, beating Bobby Allison to the line by 3.5 seconds
Rocket-powered ice sled attains 399 kph, Lake George, New York
Dan Issel (NBA-Nuggets), begins streak of 63 consecutive free throw
Ocean Ranger oil-drilling platform lost off Newfoundland, 84 die
500,000 Iranian soldiers move into Iraq
An East German sweep of the medals in the women's 3,000m speed skating at the Sarajevo Winter Olympics; Andrea Schöne takes gold ahead of teammates Karin Enke and Gabi Zange
STS 51-E vehicle moves to launch pad
Teen film "The Breakfast Club" is released, written and directed by John Hughes, starring Molly Ringwald, Judd Nelson, Emilio Estevez, Anthony Michael Hall and Ally Sheedy
44,180 largest NBA crowd to date-Philadelphia at Detroit
ABC-TV begins broadcasting "Amerika" mini-series
Karlstad skates world record 10km (14:03,92)
Nikolai Guljajev becomes world champion skater
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Israel attacks border strip Taba near Egypt
Baseball owners lock out players
Daily market volatility as Hussein mentions withdrawal, but Bush calls his offer a "cruel hoax"
Freighter with dynamite explodes in Phang Nga Thailand, 120 die
Troy State sets NCAA Div II record with 103 points in 2nd half routing DeVry Institute 187-117
100th episode of "Cops" airs on Fox
Bombings by mafia drug lords kill 14 in Bogotá, Colombia
Howard Stern Radio Show begins transmitting to Rochester, New York on WRQI 95.1 FM
Tom Barrasso becomes winningest US born goalie in NHL history, as the Pittsburgh Penguins win, 5-3 over visiting Winnipeg; passes record of 252 career wins set by Frank Brimsek
US asks Aristide to adopt a peace plan from Haiti
Burundi premier Anatole Kanyenkiko resigns
Dow Jones Industrial Average closes at record 3986.17
Population of People's Republic of China hits 1.2 billion
Mortar attack by a far left Marxist terrorist group on the US Embassy in Athens, Greece hits a parking lot wall and damages three vehicles
PKK (Kurdistan Workers Party) leader Abdullah Öcalan is arrested in Kenya
Wayne Gretzkyrecords 5 assists in NY Rangers' 7-4 win over the Predators; takes a 1,000 point lead over Gordie Howe in all time NHL scoring lead; Gretzky's only appearance in Nashville
Indian Point II nuclear power plant in New York State vents a small amount of radioactive steam when a steam generator fails
First draft of the complete human genome is published in the journal "Nature"

At the Tri-State Crematory in La Fayette, Georgia, investigators find uncremated bodies disposed of in the woods and buildings on crematorium's property. One of the worst incidents of abuse in the funeral service industry.
Kile Smith's "Symphony: Lumen ad revelationem" premieres at Muhlenberg College in Allentown, Pennsylvania, performed by the Lehigh Valley Chamber Orchestra, led by Donald Spieth
Walt Disney releases "Return to Never Land", an animated film sequel to 1953's "Peter Pan"
An estimated 6-11 million people around the world take to the streets to protest against war with Iraq
46th Daytona 500: Dale Earnhardt Jr. wins exactly 6 years to the day after his father Dale Earnhardt Sr. won his first and only Daytona 500 in 1998
51st Daytona 500: Matt Kenseth wins shortened race; called off with 48 laps to go after a severe rainstorm hits the track
Stray tabby cat Larry becomes Chief Mouser to the Cabinet Office at 10 Downing Street, the official resident of the UK Prime Minister, from Battersea Dogs & Cats Home
PhilosopherSocratesis sentenced to death by the city of Athens for corrupting the minds of the youth of the city and for impiety
Khwarezmian city of Bukhara taken byGenghis Khan's Mongol army after a 12-day siege, with the death of about 30,000 of its citizens
Christopher Columbuswrites the first account of his first voyage to the Americas in a letter to his patron Luis de Santángel, Treasurer of Aragon, who then publishes it
Colley Cibber's play "Papal Tyranny in the Reign ofKing John" premieres in London
Angelo Braschi chosen as PopePius VI
Beginning of the famous debate between French naturalistsGeorges Cuvierand Étienne Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire on animal structure
Charges of Treason againstJefferson Davisare dropped
Boer War: Siege of Kimberley broken by British troops under Lieutenant-GeneralJohn Frenchafter a 124 day siege. Kimberley defense led byCecil Rhodes.
Marconibegins regular broadcasting transmissions from Essex
Silent film "It" is released in the US starringClara Bow

As a member of the gold medal-winning US four-man bobsleigh team at Lake Placid,Eddie Eaganbecomes the only Olympian to win gold medals at both the Summer (boxing gold in Antwerp 1920) and Winter Games in different sports
George Burns &Gracie Allendebut as regulars on the "Guy Lombardo Show"
John Van Druten's play "There's Always Juliet" premieres in NYC
President-electFranklin D. Rooseveltsurvives assassination in Miami, Florida, attempt but Chicago Mayor Anton Cermak is mortally wounded, he would die on March 29

Sonja Henieof Norway becomes the most successful Olympic figure skater ever by winning her 3rd consecutive gold medal at the Garmisch-Partenkirchen Winter Games; beats Cecilia College of Great Britain by 6.4 points
Lillian Hellman's play "The Little Foxes" premieres in New York City
Duke Ellingtonand his Orchestra first record "Take the 'A' Train" written by Billy Strayhorn, at RCA Studio in Hollywood, California; it goes on to become his signature tune
Greek-American singerMaria Callasmakes her professional opera debut as Beatrice, in Franz von Suppé's "Boccaccio" at the Olympia Theatre in Athens, Greece
Mao Zedong's communist army occupies Yenan
Walt Disney's animated film "Cinderella" premieres in Boston. Massachusetts
KingGeorge VIis buried in St. George's Chapel, Windsor Castle, England
Antonio Segniforms Italian government
First US female world figure skating champion (Tenley Albright)
Bill Bradleyscores 51 points for Princeton
John Lennonpasses his driving test

John Lennonand his wife Cynthia, andGeorge Harrison, his wife Pattie, and sister-in-law Jenny, travel to India to study Transcendental Meditation for two months withMaharishi Mahesh Yogiat his ashram in Rishikesh; Paul McCartney and Ringo Starr join them a few days later, Ringo only stays for 2 weeks
Ard Schenkbecomes world champion all-round skater
Linda Ronstadttops US singles chart with 'You're No Good', and the US album chart with Heart Like A Wheel
18th Daytona 500:David Pearsonmakes contact withRichard Pettyjust yards from finish line into a wall and to infield; Petty stalls whilst Pearson restarts to win
Escaped mass murdererTed Bundyrecaptured, Pensacola, Florida
Leon SpinksbeatsMuhammad Aliin 15 for heavyweight boxing title
21st Grammy Awards:Billy Joel's "Just the Way You Are"; soundtrack to "Saturday Night Fever" featuring theBee Gees, and A Taste of Honey win
Temple City Kazoo Orchestra appears on "TheMike DouglasShow"
American speed skaterEric Heidenwins 500m in Olympic record 38.03s; first of record 5 gold medal sweep of events at Lake Placid, New York
NHL OilersWayne Gretzkyassists on NHL-record-tying 7 goals, in 8-2 victory over Washington at Northlands Coliseum, Edmonton
World chess championship match abandoned withAnatoly Karpov25 leadingGarry Kasparov23
National Assembly of the Philippines proclaimsFerdinand MarcosPresident for six more years after rigged elections againstCorazon Aquino, supercharging the EDSA people power revolution
1982 Masters champion Craig Stadler is disqualified fromAndy WilliamsOpen for kneeling on a towel to make a shot
29th Daytona 500:Bill Elliottleads for 104 of 200 laps including the last 3; had won pole with record speed of 210.364 mph
Jeffrey Dahmerfound sane and guilty of killing 15 boys
American boxerTommy Morrisonannounces he has contracted HIV, a diagnosis he later claimed to be false
NFL coaching maestroBill Belichickis fired by Cleveland, finishing his Browns coaching career with a record of 36-44
Tamil romantic comedy film "Poove Unakkaga" is released, directed by Vikraman, and starring Sangita andVijayin his breakthrough role
40th Daytona 500:Dale Earnhardt's only Daytona win in his 20th start; snaps a 59-race winless streak
7th ESPY Awards:Mark McGwire, Chamique Holdsclaw win
Martin Brodeurbecomes first goaltender in NHL history to get credit for a "game winning" goal as New Jersey wins, 4-2 over the visiting Philadelphia Flyers
Libyan protests begin opposing ColonelMuammar al-Gaddafi's rule
Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania adopt the Gregorian calendar
Adolf Hitlerannounces construction of the Volkswagen Beetle (the People's Car, aka the Käfer/Beetle)
Wartime propaganda poster "We Can Do It!" produced by J. Howard Miller and posted on the walls of Westinghouse Electric and Manufacturing Company's plants in the Midwest
Soviet military occupation of Afghanistan ends as the last Soviet troops cross the Soviet-Afghan border
The Angel of the North, a large-scale steel sculpture 20 m (66 ft) tall by Antony Gormley is installed at Gateshead, northern England
Sigismund, Holy Roman emperor (1410-37), born in Nuremberg, Kingdom of Germany (d. 1437)
Ivan the Young, Russian Prince of Moscow, heir of Ivan III of Russia, and ruler of Tver by marriage to Maria of Tver, born in Empire of Russia (d. 1490)
Piero di Lorenzo de' Medici [Piero the Unfortunate], Italian ruler of Florence (1492-94), born in Florence, Republic of Florence (d. 1503)[1]
Philipp Melanchthon, German Protestant reformer and theologian (Martin Luther's right-hand man), born in Bretten, Palatinate (d. 1560)
Juliana van Stolberg, German countess, mother of William of Orange and Jan Nassau, born at Stolberg, County of Stolberg, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1580)
Pedro Menéndez de Avilés, Spanish admiral and explorer who founded St. Augustine, Florida, born in Avilés, Spain (d. 1574)
Charles de Guise, French 2nd Cardinal de Lorraine (political influential and wealthy member of House of Guise), born in Joinville, France (d. 1574)
Alfonso Fontanelli, Italian composer (Ferrara school), born in Reggio nell'Emilia, Duchy of Modena and Reggio (d. 1622)
François Charpentier, French scholar and archaeologist, born in Paris, France (d. 1702)
Anne Jules de Noailles, French general under Louis XIV (marshal of France), born in Paris, France (d. 1708)
Antonio Maria Valsalva, Italian anatomist (anatomy of the ear), born in Imola (d. 1723)
Charles-André Van Loo, French Rococo painter, born in Nice, France (d. 1765)
Claude Prosper Jolyot de Crébillon, French satirical novelist (The Skimmer), born in Paris, France (d. 1777)
Louis XV, King of France (1715-74) known as "Louis the Beloved", born in the Palace of Versailles, France (d. 1774)
Abraham Clark, American politician, signed US Declaration of Independence, born in Elizabethtown, New Jersey (d. 1794)
Alexandre-Théodore Brongniart, French architect known for the Palais Brongniart, born in Paris (d. 1813)
Ernst Eichner, German composer and bassoonist, born in Arolsen, Landgraviate of Hesse-Darmstadt, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1777)
Bathsheba Spooner, first American woman executed after US Independence for arranging husband's murder, born in Sandwich, Province of Massachusetts Bay
Friedrich August Wolf, German philologist (Prolegomena), born in Hainrode (d. 1824)
Jean-François Le Sueur, French composer, noted for his operas and oratorios, born in Drucat-Plessiel, France (d. 1837)
Jacob Kimball, American pioneering composer, musician, and educator, born in Topsfield, Massachusetts (d. 1826) [some sources cite Feb. 22]
Jens Baggesen, Danish writer and linguist (Labyrinth), born in Korsør, Denmark (d. 1826)
Joseph Bernard Cannaert [Olim], Flemish lawyer, born in Ghent (d. 1848)
Frederick, Dutch Prince of Orange, General and Second son of King William V, born in the Hague, Netherlands (d. 1799)
Johann Nepomuk Poissl, Bavarian composer, born in Rattiszell, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1865)
Friedrich Fesca, German violinist, and composer, born in Magdeburg, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1826)
Charles Niellon, Belgian general (Liberation of Antwerp during Belgian Revolution), born in Strasbourg, France (d. 1871)
Heinrich [Henry] Engelhard Steinway, German American piano maker (Steinway & Sons), born in Wolfshagen im Harz, Duchy of Brunswick (d. 1871)
Frederick Willem Conrad, Dutch hydraulic engineer and railway pioneer (Katwyk), born near Haarlem, Netherlands (d. 1869)
John Augustus Sutter, Swiss-American immigrant on whose land the California gold rush began (New Helvetia, Sutter Mill), born in Kandern, Baden (d. 1880)
Ignacy Feliks Dobrzynski, Polish composer, born in Romanów, Russian Empire (d. 1867)
André Dumont, Belgian geologist, born in Liège, Belgium (d. 1857)
Domingo Faustino Sarmiento, President of Argentina (1868-74), born in San Juan, Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata (d. 1888)
Charles-François Daubigny, French painter of the Barbizon school, born in Paris (d. 1878)
Abraham de Amorie van der de Have, Dutch theologist and poet, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1848)
Theodor Uhlig, German composer and violinist, born in Wurzen, Kingdom of Saxony (d. 1853)
Li Hung-Tshang, Chinese modernizing politician and general (governor-general of Zhili province), born in Hefei, Anhui province, China (d. 1901)
Carter Harrison, Sr., American politician, Mayor of Chicago (1879-87, 1893) who was assassinated, born near Lexington, Kentucky (d. 1893)
George Johnstone Stoney, Irish physicist (introduced term electron), born in Oakley Park, Ireland (d. 1911)
Johan Hendrik van Dale, Dutch schoolmaster and lexicographer(Van Dale's Great Dictionary of the Dutch Language), born in Sluis, Netherlands (d. 1872)
Silas Weir Mitchell, American physician (discovered causalgia, erythromelalgia) and author (Roland Blake, Hugh Wynne), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1914)
William Henry Preece, Welsh electrical engineer/wireless pioneer, born in Caernarfon, Wales (d. 1913)
Alexander Stuart Webb, American Union general (medal for gallantry at Battle of Gettysburg), born in New York City (d. 1911)
Demetrius Vikelas, Greek businessman and writer (first President International Olympic Committee 1894-1896), born in Ermoupoli, Greece (d. 1908)
Manoel Ferraz de Campos Salles, Brazilian lawyer, politician (President of Brazil, 1898-1902), and coffee grower, born in Campinas, São Paulo, Brazil (d. 1913)
Robert Fuchs, Austrian composer, and teacher (Vienna Conservatory, 1875-1912), born in Frauental, Austria (d. 1927)
Gustav Hollaender, German violinist, conductor, and composer, born in Leobschütz, Germany (d. 1915)
William Henry Pickering, American astronomer (9th & 10th moons of Saturn), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1938)
Halford John Mackinder, British geographer (Britain and the British Seas), born in Gainsborough, Lincolnshire, England (d. 1947)
Bannister Fletcher, English architect and architectural historian (A History of Architecture), born in London (d. 1953)
Hans von Euler-Chelpin, German-born Swedish biochemist who investigated the fermentation of sugar and enzymes (Nobel 1929), born in Augsburg, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1964)
Emilis Melngailis, Latvian composer and ethnomusicologist, born in Vidriži, Russian Empire (d. 1954)
Ernest Wilson, English botanist (plant collector in China), born in Chipping Campden, England (d. 1930)
Sax Rohmer [Arthur Henry Sarsfield Ward], English author (Dr Fu Manchu novels), born in Birmingham, England (d. 1959)
Richard Würz, German composer, and music critic, born in Munich, Germany (d. 1965)
H. M. Bateman, British cartoonist (The Man Who... series), born in Sutton Forest, Australia (d. 1970)
Robert Ley, German Nazi politician, born in Niederbreidenbach, Germany (d. 1945)
Semyon Bogatyrev, Russian and Soviet musicologist and composer (completed a symphony abandoned by Piotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky), born in Kharkiv, Ukraine, Russian Empire (d. 1960)
James Forrestal, American banker and administrator (Secretary of the Navy, 1944-47, 1st US Secretary of Defense 1947-49), born in Beacon, New York (d. 1949)[1]
Harm Kamerlingh Onnes, Dutch painter, etcher and ceramist, born in Zoeterwoude, Netherlands (d. 1985)
Walter Donaldson, American pianist and popular song composer ("Makin' Whoopee"; "My Blue Heaven"; My Buddy"), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1947)
Oswaldo Aranha, Brazilian politician and statesman (President of the UN General Assembly 1948-49), born in Alegrete, Brazil (d. 1960)
Arthur Shields, Irish actor ("The River"; "Enchanted Island"), born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 1970)
Earl Blaik, American College Football coach (NCAA C'ship 1944–46; AFCA Coach of the Year 1946; Army) and executive (AD Army 1948–59, born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1989)
Totò [Antonio Clemente], Italian actor (Motorizzate, Noi Duri) commonly referred to as the most popular Italian comedian of all time, born in Naples, Italy (d. 1967)
Gale Sondergaard, American actress (The Letter, Road to Rio), born in Litchfield, Minnesota (d. 1985)
Georges Auric, French composer (one of Les Six), born in Lodève, Hérault, France (d. 1983)
Lillian Disney, wife of animator Walt Disney, born in Spalding, Idaho (d. 1997)
George Earnshaw, American baseball pitcher (World Series 1929-30; Philadelphia Athletics), born in Little Rock, Arkansas (d. 1976)
Andre Parrot, French archaeologist (Mari, Assur), born in Désandans, France (d. 1980)
Christmas Humphreys, English lawyer and prominent British Buddhist (Awakening of Zen), born in Ealing, England (d. 1983)
James "Kokomo" Arnold, American blues slide guitarist, and singer, born in Lovejoy's Station, Georgia (d. 1968) [some sources give birth year as 1896]
Paul Haesaerts, Belgian architect and painter (Animisme), born in Boom, Belgium (d. 1974)
Antonin Magne, French cyclist (Tour de France 1931, 34), born in Ytrac, France (d. 1983)
Mary Adshead, English muralist and painter, born in London, England (d. 1995)
Nise da Silveira, Brazilian psychiatrist (made advances in occupational therapy), born in Maceió, Brazil (d. 1999)
Jean Langlais, French organist, and composer (Missa Salve Regina), born in La Fontenelle, Brittany, France (d. 1991)
Sarto Fournier, Quebec politician, Mayor of Montreal (1957-60), born in East Broughton, Quebec  (d. 1980)
Sidney Gilliat, English screenwriter (Night Train to Munich) and director, born in Stockport, Cheshire, England (d. 1994)
Ypk van der Fear, Dutch writer (Reade Runen), born in Sondel, Netherlands (d. 1983)
Harold Beeley, British diplomat to the Arab world, born in Manchester, England (d. 2001)
Miep Gies, Dutch discoverer of Anne Frank's diary and friend of the Frank family, born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 2010)
Irena Sendler [née Krzyżanowska], Polish nurse, social worker, and WWII heroine who helped smuggle Jewish ghetto children to safety, born in Warsaw, Russian Poland (d. 2008)[1]
Leonard Woodcock, American labor leader (United Auto Workers) and US ambassador to China, born in Providence, Rhode Island (d. 2001)
George Mikes, Hungarian-British writer and humorist (How to Be an Alien), born in Siklós, Hungary (d. 1987)
Erich Eliskases, Austrian chess player (grandmaster 1952), born in Innsbruck, Austria (d. 1997)
Willy Vandersteen, Belgian cartoonist (Suske & Wiske), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1990)
(Thomas) Hale Boggs, American politician (US Representative from Louisiana, 1947-73), born in Long Beach, Mississippi (d. 1972)
Kevin McCarthy, American actor (Invasion of Body Snatchers, Death of a Salesman), born in Seattle, Washington (d. 2010)
Ian Ballantine, American publisher (Ballantine Books), born in New York City (d. 1995)
Mary Jane Croft, American actress (Our Miss Brooks: The Lucy Show), born in Muncie, Indiana (d. 1999)
Ronnie Aldrich, British easy listening and jazz pianist, arranger, and composer, born in Erith, Kent, England (d. 1993)
Allan Arbus, American commercial photographer, and actor (M*A*S*H - "Dr. Sidney Freeman"; Greaser's Palace; Curb Your Enthusiasm), born in New York City (d. 2013)
Endicott Peabody, American politician, 62nd Governor of Massachusetts (1963-65), born in Lawrence, Massachusetts (d. 1997)
Ingmar Milveden, Swedish organist, composer, and musicologist, born in Gothenburg, Sweden (d. 2007)
Piet van Aken, Flemish writer (Klinkaart, Het begeren), born in Terhagen, Flanders, Belgium (d. 1984)
Richard O'Brien, British Industrial relations expert (Manpower Services Commission), born in Chesterfield, England (d. 2009)
Radha Krishna Choudhary, Indian historian and writer (History of Bihar), born in Begusarai, India (d. 1985)[1][2]
Herman Kahn, American, writer and military strategist (Thinking About the Unthinkable), born in Bayonne, New Jersey (d. 1983)
John B. Anderson, American politician and 1980 Republican presidential candidate, born in Rockford, Illinois (d. 2017)
Keene Curtis, American character actor (The Rothschilds, Cheers, Amanda's), born in Salt Lake City, Utah (d. 2002)
Frank Dunlop, British theater director (Edinburgh International Festival, 1984-91), born in Leeds, England
Gottfrid Gräsbeck, Finnish choral director, and composer, born in Turku, Finland (d. 2010)
Harvey Korman, American actor (The Carol Burnett Show; Blazing Saddles; High Anxiety), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2008)
(Mary) Norma Procter, British contralto singer, born in Cleethorpes, England (d. 2017)
Norman Bridwell, American author and cartoonist (Clifford the Big Red Dog), born in Kokomo, Indiana (d. 2014)
